coredata.dc:title: The MOF-originated NiCu-POx/NGQD for efficient supercapattery devices and hydrogen evolution reaction

coredata.dc:description: Due to the high viability of electrochemical energy resources and their storage techniques in the present climate, supercapacitors and water-splitting electrolysis have seen dramatically increased attention in modern days. We presented the synthesis of nickel-copper phosphate (NiCu-POx) nanosheets from the phosphorization of the nickel-copper metal-organic framework (NiCu-MOF) at a limited temperature. Further, the addition of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQD) in the Ni–Cu-POx was also studied. SEM, XRD, and XPS were used to analyze the structural and elemental composition of the synthesized materials. Further, Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) measurements showed an enhancement in surface area when NGQDs were introduced into the heterostructure of NiCu-POx. An extraordinary value of specific capacity (1431 C-g−1 at 1.4 Ag-1) was achieved with NiCu-POx/NGQDs. Supercapattery device (NiCu-POx/NGQDs//PANI@AC) was fabricated with polyaniline doped activated carbon as the negative and NiCu-POx/NGQDs as the positive electrode. The specific capacity of the real device (NiCu-POx/NGQDs//PANI@AC) was 224 C-g−1 at 2.0 Ag-1. The energy density for this asymmetric device (NiCu-POx/NGQDs//PANI@AC) was 73 Whkg−1 at 980 Wkg-1 power density. The device (NiCu-POx/NGQDs//PANI@AC) was subjected to 10,000 charge-discharge cycles to test its durability. After 10,000 cycles NiCu-POx/NGQDs//PANI@AC demonstrates an outstanding 86% reservation of capacity. The NiCu-POx/NGQDs showed a small overpotential of 138 mV during the 24 h HER test. According to our findings, NiCu-POx/NGQDs proved to be a suitable electrode material for prospective supercapattery and HER applications.

head.ce:title: The MOF-originated NiCu-POx/NGQD for efficient supercapattery devices and hydrogen evolution reaction

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to the high viability of electrochemical energy resources and their storage techniques in the present climate, supercapacitors and water-splitting electrolysis have seen dramatically increased attention in modern days. We presented the synthesis of nickel-copper phosphate (NiCu-POx) nanosheets from the phosphorization of the nickel-copper metal-organic framework (NiCu-MOF) at a limited temperature. Further, the addition of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQD) in the Ni–Cu-POx was also studied. SEM, XRD, and XPS were used to analyze the structural and elemental composition of the synthesized materials. Further, Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) measurements showed an enhancement in surface area when NGQDs were introduced into the heterostructure of NiCu-POx. An extraordinary value of specific capacity (1431 C-g−1 at 1.4 Ag-1) was achieved with NiCu-POx/NGQDs. Supercapattery device (NiCu-POx/NGQDs//PANI@AC) was fabricated with polyaniline doped activated carbon as the negative and NiCu-POx/NGQDs as the positive electrode. The specific capacity of the real device (NiCu-POx/NGQDs//PANI@AC) was 224 C-g−1 at 2.0 Ag-1. The energy density for this asymmetric device (NiCu-POx/NGQDs//PANI@AC) was 73 Whkg−1 at 980 Wkg-1 power density. The device (NiCu-POx/NGQDs//PANI@AC) was subjected to 10,000 charge-discharge cycles to test its durability. After 10,000 cycles NiCu-POx/NGQDs//PANI@AC demonstrates an outstanding 86% reservation of capacity. The NiCu-POx/NGQDs showed a small overpotential of 138 mV during the 24 h HER test. According to our findings, NiCu-POx/NGQDs proved to be a suitable electrode material for prospective supercapattery and HER applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Because of the depletion of fossil-fuel resources, it is essential to develop renewable energy sources and conversion techniques for energy storage. Water electrolysis is a reliable and effective way of producing environmentally friendly hydrogen fuel. Researchers have been attracted to supercapacitors (SCs) and water splitting because electrochemical energy storage technologies and conversion procedures are now reasonably practicable. Noble metals often act as electrocatalysts, but the high price of rare metals constraints their practical application. In this context, efforts are focused on phosphides, metal oxides, and chalcogenides as suitable electrocatalysts in water-splitting processes. However, their vast practical applications are severely constrained because of their significant overpotentials for the electrochemical reaction [1].

Energy storage devices (ESD) can be operated along with these renewable energy resources to overcome the energy crisis. Batteries and capacitors are widely used as energy storage devices nowadays. The asymmetric SCs developed using different kinds of electrodes, also known as supercapattery. Supercapattery devices are attaining popularity since it combines the characteristics of both batteries and supercapacitors. The integration of two distinct technologies onto a single device significantly improves performance. Much more study is required to find suitable materials for electrode preparation in supercappatery applications [2].

Metal phosphates are extensively used as an electroactive candidate in SCs and lithium batteries due to their metal-like properties, plenty of active sites, high electrical conductivity, and powerful structural stability. When implemented as electrodes for SCs, these features cause phosphides to have enhanced capacities and satisfactory rate performances. Therefore many phosphates such as MoP [3], Fe3PO4 [4], CoPO4 [5], and NiPO4 [6]were widely employed for SC applications. The binary metallic phosphates had sufficiently higher performance in SCs than single metallic phosphate, as Chen et al. [7] reported earlier. Ni–Co–P had a capacity of 550 Cg-1, which was more than individual nickel and cobalt phosphate capacity.

Due to their exceptionally high surface areas and preternatural porosities, The ideal starting materials for the synthesis of electroactive materials for SCs are metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), a class of crystalline phase that self-assembles with metallic ions (or metal aggregates) and organic linkers [8]. The different kinds of MOF based on oxides [9], sulfides [10], and phosphates [11] have been tested for SCs applications till now. But the MOF-based binary phosphates are rarely reported in the past [12,13].

Nickel phosphate as an electrode for supercapattery applications is generally available at a reasonable price and has good enactment. Nickel phosphate has an inorganic nickel metal structure with a phosphate tetrahedron with four oxygen atoms at its corners, forming Ni–O–P connections. Strong P–O bonds have an inductive impact on the redox potential, increasing energy density. Amorphous nickel phosphate has a larger energy density than crystalline nickel phosphate because of the large number of pores and active sites accessible in the amorphous state, resulting in more structural defects [14–16]. When coupled with other metal phosphates, nickel phosphate.

As a result, highly crystalline graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with a few nanometers are suitable candidates to enhance the electrochemical performance of MOF-doped phosphates. They can aid in overcoming the drawbacks of present techniques. According to reports, the higher performance of GQDs is because of their unique surface characteristics, which include an abundance of active sites for redox reactions, which improve their interactions with the electrolyte ions. That undoubtedly enhances the specific capacitance, cyclic stability, and rate performance of SCs [17–19].

Our work demonstrates the effect of combining NGQDs with MOF-doped NiCu-POx. The hydrothermal method was applied to synthesize NiCu-MOF, NiCu-POx, and NiCu-POx/NGQDs. The performance of MOF-doped NiCu-POx/NGQDs heterostructures was tested in asymmetric SCs. Although NiCu-POx has been reported as a supercapattery electrode material, the effect of introducing NGQDs into NiCu-POx heterostructure is limited. So, NGQDs were combined with NiCu-POx and applied in a three-cell assembly to check their electrochemical behavior. X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscope (SEM), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were used to study structure, morphology, and composition. The electrochemical characteristics of all the synthesized samples were analyzed by performing electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD), and cyclic voltammetry (CV) in an electrolytic solution of 1 M KOH. We explore the characteristics of a supercapattery (NiCu-POx/NGQDs//PANI@AC) using polyaniline doped activated carbon as the cathode rather than single activated carbon. Since prior research has focused on battery-graded electrode alteration in supercapattery design. However, both capacitive and battery-graded electrodes were modified in this work. This hybrid device was subjected to additional stability testing involving 10,000 continuous cycles. After 10,000 cycles, the capacity retention and columbic efficiency for supercapattery (NiCu-POx/NGQDs//PANI@AC) were measured.

Sigma Aldrich supplied potassium hydroxide (KOH), carbon black, hydrochloric acid (HCl), N-methyl 2- pyrrolidone (NMP), 2,5-dihydroxyterephthalic acid (H4-DHBDC), nickel (II) nitrate (NiNO3·6H2O), copper (II) nitrate (CuNO3·6H2O), 3-aminopropyl)-triethoxysilane (APTES), activated carbon, urea, poly (vinylpyrrolidone) (PVP), citric acid, and N-polyvinylidene-fluoride (PVDF). DUKSAN PURE CHEMICALS was chosen to supply sodium phosphate dibasic anhydrous (Na2HPO4). Urich technology provided nickel foam (NF). Deionized water (DI) was utilized to prepare all sorts of aqueous solutions for the testing. Since all chemicals were purchased in pure form, there is no need for further purification to synthesize nickel-copper phosphate. ALS Co., Ltd. provided the Hg/HgO and Pt wire used as a reference and counter cell in three-cell setups.

Two solutions were produced by dissolving 1.52 g of citric acid and 1.82 g of urea into 40 ml of DI water. The citric acid solution was placed on a hot stirrer while another solution was added with a pipette. The combined solution was stirred continuously for 45 min. After that, this solution was heated in a furnace at 150 °C for 10 h. The unwanted impurities were removed by washing with ethanol. A total of 0.4 mg/ml of NGQDs were dispersed in ethanol [20,21].

The hydrothermal technique was chosen because of its simplicity in producing the desired crystalline materials. Unlike other synthetic procedures, such as chemical precipitation and sonochemical, this method can be utilized to provide the ability to synthesize crystalline materials at high temperatures. First, 0.290 g of NiNO3·6H2O, 0.125 g of CuNO3·6H2O, 1.35 g of poly (vinylpyrrolidone) (PVP), and 55 mg of 2,5-dihydroxyterephthalic acid (H4-DHBDC) were dispersed in 30 ml of ethanol: N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF): DI water (1:1:1 vol ratio). After 45 min of stirring, the whole solution was transferred to a Teflon autoclave and placed in the furnace at 150 °C for 10 h. After then, it was gradually cooled to ambient temperature. The prepared material was thoroughly cleaned with DIW and ethanol. This sample might have contained water particles, which were eliminated by heating at 55 °C for 2–4 h.

The phosphorization of NiCu-MOF under an Ar atmosphere in a tube furnace was used to synthesize the NiCu-POx. The 25 mg of NiCu-MOF and 110 mg of sodium hydrogen phosphate dibasic anhydrate (Na2HPO4) were put separately in combustion boats. The pure Ar gas was entered into the tube. The tube with NiCu-MOF and Na2HPO4 precursors was heated at 300 °C for 3 h.

The Ni–Cu-POx heterostructure was prepared by APTES coating on Ni–Cu-POx surface. First, NiCu-POx were washed with DIW and dried at 60 °C for 2–3 h 4 ml of APTES were dissolved in 50 ml of dehydrated toluene in an ordinary run. A small amount of Ni–Cu-POx powder, about 0.6 g, was added, and the solution was ultrasonically mixed for 40 min. At this time, evacuation preparation may be performed. To guarantee an atmosphere of dryness in the refluxing framework, a CaCl2 drying tube was used at the end. The solid phase of the resulting mixture was filtered, thoroughly washed using fresh toluene a total of six times, and then dehydrated overnight at 120 °C for additional curing. The fresh toluene was used to wash and remove the extra APTES. The material was dried at 60 °C for 8 h and then used further [22,23].

The NGQDs were added into NiCu-POx material through the EDC/NHS reaction between the amine group of (3-aminopropyl)-triethoxysilane APTES and a carboxylic acid group of N-GQDs. 4 mg of N-(3-(dimethylamino)-propyl)-N′-ethyl carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and 5 mg of N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were added into 10 ml of DIW and placed on a stirrer for 4 h. To eliminate undesirable particles, the nanomaterials were cleaned with DIW and methanol. Fig. 1 illustrates the hydrothermal technique to synthesize the NiCu-POx/NGQDs.

The polymerization of aniline was used to synthesize polyaniline (PANI). 0.25 M aniline was put into a 40 ml hydrochloric acid (HCl) solution. After that, 60 ml of HCl was used to dissolve the ammonium persulfate solution (APS). After that ammonium persulfate (APS) solution in HCl was added to the aniline solution, and continual stirring began. The color of the solution changes from pale orange to dark greenish. The prepared solution was stirred all night, while the colloidal solution was rinsed many times in a centrifuge with DIW and acetone. The synthesized PANI was then combined in a 50/50 wt ratio with activated carbon. In 10 ml of DI water, 0.75 mg of PANI and 0.75 mg of activated carbon were dissolved separately. The solution of PANI was put on a magnetic stirrer, and activated carbon was added drop by drop while the solutions were constantly stirred for 25 min. The above procedure was used to synthesize PANI-doped activated carbon for two cell setup.

The Bruker D8 Advance Cu Kα radiation (1.54) was used to compute X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns. Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, JEOL JSM-7600 F) were used to analyze the morphological and structural properties of the samples. The nitrogen adsorption/desorption characteristics curve was studied using a Quantachrome Autosorb AS-6B device. A Bruker Vertex 70 spectrometer was used to obtain Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). Renishaw InVia Reflex Raman was applied to collect the Raman spectra. TGA 3000 (Thermogravimetric Analyzer) was applied to study the thermal stability and volatile components.

With the use of the Corrtest CS300 Potentiostat, CV, EIS, and GCD measurements were taken to study the electrochemical characteristics of the NiCu-MOF, NiCu-POx, and NiCu-POx/NGQDs. The Horiba SA-9600 was used to determine surface area by calculating Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) readings. NF was covered with an active material to fabricate electrodes which were then operated in a three-cell arrangement. Other electrodes (counter and reference) were made of platinum and Hg/HgO, respectively. As an electrolytic solution between these electrodes, we prepared a 1 M solution of KOH by dissolving 2.85 g of KOH pellets in 50 ml of DI water. To prepare the slurry for working electrode preparation, 80% of the active material was mixed with 10% carbon black using a 10% PVDF binder. After that, we stirred this mixture for 8 h to prepare a homogeneous solution. Nickel foam was covered with this slurry. Nickel foam was cleaned first with hydrochloric acid (HCl), acetone, ethanol, and deionized H2O and then used. This slurry was deposited over NF in a 1 × 1 cm2 region. The total active material mass transferred on NF was 5–6 mg. The steps outlined above were also done for NiCu-MOF, NiCu-POx, and NiCu-POx/NGQDs. In addition, for the supercapattery application, PANI@AC and NiCu-POx/NGQDs served as the negative and positive terminals. The operating voltage of 0–0.6 V was maintained throughout all the calculations. All CV calculations were taken at various scans (3–50 mVs−1). Similarly, GCD calculations were performed at various values of currents ranging from (1.0–2.0 A g-1). Stability tests were conducted under room conditions using 10,000 repetitions of charging/discharging. CV profiles can estimate specific capacity using the equation [24].

The active sites and morphology directly impact the electrochemical responsiveness and stability of the electrodes. The FTIR spectra of NGQDs were represented in Fig. 2 (a). The wide band absorption at 300–350 cm−2 can be due to O–H and N–H stretching vibration bonds. The peak represented the absorption band of C = O in COOH at 1667 cm−1 [52]. While the peak at 1575 cm−1 was due to the C = C bending vibration, and 1401 cm−1 was due to the C–N bending vibration. The outcome demonstrates that the surface of NGQDs had a lot of amine and hydroxyl groups because the N source was provided by urea for doping. The cause of favorable hydrophilic surface characteristics of NGQDs may be the nitrogenous and oxygenated group.

Furthermore, the alteration in the graphitic structure of the NGQDs was detected using Raman spectroscopy. The crystalline (G) and disordered (D) bands were shown in Fig. 2(b) at 1575 cm −1 and 1378 cm−1, respectively. The G band represents the in-plane vibration of sp2 carbon, whereas the D band was connected to the existence of sp3 defects [22]. The intensity of ID/IG can be applied to link the structural properties. The value of ID/IG was 0.88 for NGQDs, which was evidence of their crystalline structure [22].
X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to further explore the electronic structure and doping distribution of NGQDs. Fig. 2(c) displays the full-scan XPS spectra of NGQDs.

The three spikes originated at 286.1, 399.6, and 532.1 eV. The spike at 286.1 eV corresponds to C 1s, 399.6 eV corresponds to N 1s, and 532.1 eV corresponds to O 1s. The O/C atomic ratio for NGQDs was measured to be 67.9%, which belongs to their oxidation behaviour, also linked with the prior investigation [26]. The nitrogen contamination was up to 4.1%, similar to the amount of NGQDs reported earlier [27]. Fig. 2(d) displays the high-resolution N 1s spectra of NGQDs, which exhibits simultaneously pyrrolic at BE of 400.5 eV and pyridine at 399.3 eV, which were identical to NGQDs [28,29]. Fig. 2(e) displays the strong C 1s spectra of NGQDs. After deconvolution treatment shows five peaks at 284.6, 286.0, 286.5, 287.4, and 288.7 eV. The C–C sp2 bonded carbon group belongs to 284.6 eV; the C–OH hydroxyl group belongs to 286.0 eV, the C–N group belongs to 286.5 eV; C = O carbonyl group belongs to 287.4 eV, and C(O)–O carboxyl groups belongs to 288.7 eV [30]. It has been observed that NGQDs were extremely stable and soluble in water because they contain functional groups, including hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl. It was observed that the NGQDs solution had a long-lasting homogeneous phase lasting longer than six months at room temperature without even the slightest sign of disintegration. XRD pattern for NGQDs was also provided in supplementary portion by Fig. S1.

The morphological properties were studied using scanning electron microscopy. The crystallite size calculated from these SEM images was 50–70 nm. The SEM image of NiCu-MOF, NiCu-POx, and NiCu-POx/NGQDs displayed nanosheet-like morphology as indicated in Fig. 3 (a–c). NGQDs were linked to the APTES-coated material NiCu-POx when EDC/NHS was involved to bind the NGQDs to the NiCu-POx. APTES was initially applied to the NiCu-POx. Because NGQDs include many carboxylic groups, the amino units of APTES-coated NiCu-POx can immediately react with the COOH segment of NGQDs, forming an amide bond, allowing NGQDs to be tightly attached to the surface of NiCu-POx, [31]. NGQDs were equally dispersed and concentrated on the surface of the NiCu-POx, as demonstrated by the surface morphology of NiCu-POx/NGQDs in Fig. 3(c).

The crystallinity of NiCu-MOF, NiCu-POx, and NiCu-POx/NGQDs was investigated with X-ray diffraction and displayed in Fig. 3(d and e). Scherer's formula was used to compute the crystallite size of NiCu-MOF, NiCu-POx, and NiCu-POx/NGQDs.
(4)

K λ
β

θ

The crystallite size was represented by D, λ represented wavelength, the dimensional shape was denoted by k, the beta was the line width (FWHM),
θ
was the Bragg diffraction angle. NiCu-MOF, NiCu-POx, and NiCu-POx/NGQDs have 50, 56, and 64 nm crystalline sizes, respectively. The XRD pattern of NiCu-MOF is represented in Fig. 3(d). The diffraction spikes for Ni-MOF and Cu- MOF match with Shete et al. [32] and Saitou et al. [33] corresponding to the JCPDS no. 04–0836 and 04–0850. Peaks appeared at 45.2°, and 46.7° belongs to Cu (111) and Ni (111) planes. And peaks that appeared at two theta value, 50.3° and 51°, belongs to the Cu (200) and Ni (200) plane, which confirms the polycrystalline behaviour of Ni and Cu. The diffraction pattern for NiCu-POx and NiCu-POx/NGQDs were represented in Fig. 3(e). The peaks appeared at 2
θ
= 23.69°, 30.9°, 39.12°, and 47.32° corresponding to the (101), (−301), (−112)and (411), respectively, diffraction planes match with JCPDS-33-951 proving the presence of Ni3 (PO4)2·8H2O [34]. Peaks appeared at 2
θ
= 12.8°, 16.23°, 18.32°, 34.43°, 37.64°, and 54.14° corresponds to diffraction planes (001), (110), (011), (221), (031), and (322) which confirms the presence of Cu2PO4(O·H.), match with JCPDS-01-083-1557 [35]. Characteristic peaks show no change after treatment with APTES. A modest rise in intensity level can be seen probably due to the well-dispersion of NiCu-POx following APTES coating. When NGQDs were attached to APTES-coated NiCu-POx, a wide hump over 29.2° emerged, which confirms the successful addition of NGQDs into the heterostructure of NiCu-POx [36].

The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out between 30 and 800 °C for the NiCu-MOF, APTES-coated NiCu-POx, and NiCu-POx/NGQDs. NiCu-MOF shows a modest loss in weight at 25–170 °C, as illustrated in Fig. 3(f), which was related to the loss of internal and surface-adsorbed water molecules. The main cause of the rapid weight loss between 400 and 510 °C temperature range in NiCu-MOF was the breakdown of rare organic constituents and hydroxyl groups [37]. When APTES-coated NiCu-POx was heated to 300–510 °C, a gradual weight loss occurred mostly because of the pyrolysis of hydrogenated and oxygenated units [23]. Notably, the overall weight loss of the APTES-coated NiCu-POx was 20%, larger than that of the NiCu-POx. The enhanced weight loss confirmed APTES activity in nanoparticles after the thermal treatment. Similarly, NiCu-POx/NGQDs exhibit a constant weight loss from 30 to 800 °C with a total weight loss of 21.4 wt%. Due to the addition of NGQDs to APTES-coated NiCu-POx nanocomposites, 1.4% weight loss increases.

To gain detailed information about elemental composition, the XPS was performed, and the findings were illustrated in Fig. 4 (a–c). The complete XPS spectrum for NiCu-POx/NGQDs were represented in supplementary portion by Fig. S1(b). Fig. 4(a) shows two strong XPS peaks, which appeared at a binding energy of 855.6 and 871.1 eV, indicating the presence of Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2, respectively [38]. More structure of Cu 2p displays two peaks at 933 and 944 eV binding energy assigned to Cu 2p3/2, and two peaks appeared at 953.5 and 962.5 eV, which belong to Cu 2p1/2 as shown in Fig. 4(b) [39]. Similarly, two peaks at 129.7 eV and 134.0 eV match P 2p, as indicated in Fig. 4(c) [40].

To study the conductance of NiCu-MOF, NiCu-POx, and NiCu-POx/NGQDs, as well as to comprehend the chemical reactions that occur at the interface, EIS measurements were performed within a frequency limit of 0.1–100 kHz. As can be seen from Fig. 4(d), the Nyquist charts for NiCu-MOF, NiCu-POx, and NiCu-POx/NGQDs deviate from ordinary semicircular shape, which refers to the unequal electrochemical activity may be due to the adjustment of electric field and roughness of electrode surface. Equivalent series resistance (ESR) was defined as series resistance at the contact of electrode and electrolyte [41]. These nyquist graphs were used to calculate ESR at the junction of higher frequency along the x-axis. ESR is also used to describe total resistances, which include current collecting resistance, connection resistance, and electrode resistance. The measured ESR values for NiCu-MOF, NiCu-POx, and NiCu-POx/NGQDs were 1.15 U, 1.04 U, and 0.98 U, respectively.

The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) technique was applied to study the specific surface area (SSA) by adsorbing nitrogen gas constantly adsorbed over a solid sample. Because of the weak forces (van der Waals) that generate a monolayer of gas, the molecule's adsorption on the gas surface was limited. The adsorption rate may be used to compute surface area. BET calculations were performed to estimate the surface area, size of pores, and pore volume of NiCu-MOF, NiCu-POx, and NiCu-POx/NGQDs. Fig. 4(e–g) shows that the form of the BET isotherm curves was identical to an IV-type graph. The NiCu-MOF SSA, pore diameter, and pore volume were calculated to be 43.22 m2g-1, 28.65 nm, and 0.13 cm3, respectively, while for NiCu-POx were 49.17 m2g-1, 32 nm, and 0.19 cm3, respectively. Introducing NGQDs into the heterostructure of NiCu-POx improves the surface area. The calculated surface area, pore size, and pore volume for NiCu-POx/NGQDs were 51.15 m2g-1, 33 nm, and 0.23 cm3. The large surface area of NiCu-POx/NGQDs was responsible for its high electrochemical performance. All of these factors were mentioned in Table 1 . The mesoporous structure of NiCu-POx improved the electrochemical storage capacity. These findings imply that a bigger surface area allowed for a greater number of charge accumulations, and larger volume allowed faster ion transport [42].

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) is a method for evaluating the electrochemical properties of nanomaterials. In cyclic voltammetry, the form of the CV curves was used to describe the characteristics of the material. Additionally, the capacity to hold charges by the electrodes was also evaluated using CV charts. If the negative polarity was raised, they eventually reached a point where there were significantly fewer electrons in the liquid than in the electrode. As a consequence, electrons move from the electrode to the solution in a process known as reduction current (or from greater to lower potential). In this case the main cause of faradaic current was the reduction process. If the positive potential was raised, at a certain point, there will be more electrons in the solution than on the electrode. The electrons were consequently transferred from the solution to the electrode. The current flowing due to this movement of electrons is known as the oxidation current [43]. CV measurements were used to study the electrochemical characteristics of the synthesized NiCu-MOF, NiCu-POx, and NiCu-POx/NGQDs using operating potential (OP) of 0.6 V, and the results were given in Fig. 5 (a–c). The shape of the CV curve for the ideal EDLC was rectangular, while for PCs the CV curves show small faradaic peaks along the rectangular pattern. The electrode substance has a better catalytic activity during the charge storing mechanism, according to the closeness of the redox reaction peaks. These points provide information about the origin of the faradaic reactions. The mechanism is reversible if the redox peaks coincide; if there is a slight difference, the mechanism will be semi-reversible; and if the peaks diverge, the mechanism is only irreversible. These materials have faradaic and nonrectangular CV shapes, which indicates that they were battery graded. The scattering of electrolytes was further linked to redox peaks, indicating that NiCu-MOF, NiCu-POx, and NiCu-POx/NGQDs exhibited battery-like characteristics [44]. The synthesized material NiCu-POx was manually mixed with NGQDs to improve the performance. The operational potential of 0.6 V was maintained, and CV calculations were taken at variable values of scan rates 3 to 50 mVs−1. The electrochemical activity for binary NiCu-POx/NGQDs increases due to the synergetic impact of NiCu-POx and NGQDs [45].

The combined impact of NGQDs and NiCu-POx increases the active sites, surface area, and the number of pores, all of which result in the enhancement of electrochemical activity. These findings show that combining NGQDs and NiCu-POx improves the surface area of materials and their conductivity which are the two important qualities for electrochemical properties. The area under the CV curve increases with an increase in scan rate [46]. Fig. 5(d) explains the CV comparison for NiCu-MOF, NiCu-POx, and NiCu-POx/NGQDs at a scan rate of 3 mVs−1. Fig. 5(d) confirms that material NiCu-POx/NGQDs occupies dominant redox spikes. The charge storage on electrodes increases as the area under CV curves increases. As shown in Fig. 5(d), NiCu-POx/NGQDs have a wider area as compared to NiCu-MOF and NiCu-POx, demonstrating outstanding electroactive behaviour. The charge storage process was examined using the b-fitting value in Fig. S2.

For additional confirmation that NiCu-POx/NGQDs show battery-type behaviour, the peak currents were displayed along the root of scanning speed. The results were explained in the supplementary portion by Fig. S3.

GCD calculations were used to investigate the electrochemical characteristics of NiCu-MOF, NiCu-POx, and NiCu-POx/NGQDs. These results confirmed that NiCu-POx/NGQDs was the best tuned among the others. Fig. 6 (a–c) shows GCD profiles for all materials (NiCu-MOF, NiCu-POx, and NiCu-POx/NGQDs) at 1.0–3.0 A g-1 currents. The start and the end point of GCD curves exhibit a straight line, due to the formation of a double layer at the interaction of electrolyte/electrode. However, a redox reaction can be observed in the nonlinear part of the graphs [47]. The GCD curves for all materials displayed nonlinear behaviour, showing that intrinsic and matrix materials were battery graded [48]. The diffusion-controlled charge storage process enables the GCD profile to diverge significantly from the normal triangular shape of EDLC. The graph showed that an increase in current density decreases discharge time, indicating an inverse relationship between specific capacity and current densities [49]. At lower current densities, the interaction time of hydroxide ions with the surface of the electrode was large. Therefore specific capacity was high at low currents. A comparison of GCD curves for all materials was shown in Fig. 6(d), which confirms that NiCu-POx/NGQDs had a longer discharge time than others.

The long discharge time of NiCu-POx/NGQDs heterostructure indicated the highest capacity of NiCu-POx/NGQDs. The longer discharge duration of NiCu-POx/NGQDs than other synthesized materials could be due to a large region exposed to OH− ions. This causes fast reversible faradaic processes on the surface of the NiCu-POx/NGQDs electrode. The synchronous activity of NiCu-POx/NGQDs, which offers a platform for a large number of charge transportation, also contributed to the improvement in discharge slope. The NiCu-MOF, NiCu-POx, and NiCu-POx/NGQDs electrodes behaved as battery-grade materials, as shown by the correlated GCD and CV data. The concurrent GCD curves of NiCu-POx/NGQDs showed the optimum oxidation/reduction characteristics and good capacity because of the prolonged discharge timespan. The superb conductivity and relatively low resistance for all materials also contributed to less IR loss, even at very high densities [50]. The finding implied that the material has notable connected redox peaks during charge-discharge procedures, indicating faradaic reactions.

With the help of equation (4), specific capacity was estimated through CV for NiCu-MOF, NiCu-POx, and NiCu-POx/NGQDs. Specific capacities determined from CV measurements in the three-cell assembly for NiCu-MOF, NiCu-POx and NiCu-POx/NGQDs at various scans were displayed in Fig. 7 (a). The estimated capacity for NiCu-MOF and NiCu-POx was 515.34 and 804.12 C-g−1, respectively at a 3 mVs−1 scan graphically demonstrated in Fig. 7(b). The sample NiCu-POx/NGQDs storage capability was the highest, with a specific capacity of 1320 C-g−1. Similarly, capacities estimated through GCD for all materials were shown in Fig. 7(c). The capacity through GCD was calculated from equation (5) for NiCu-MOF, NiCu-POx, and NiCuPOx/NGQDs. The capacities calculated at a 1.4 A g-1 current for NiCu-MOF, NiCu-POx, and NiCu-POx/NGQDs were 560.5, 878.2, and 1431 C-g−1, respectively expressed graphically in Fig. 7(d). The highest specific capacity achieved for NiCu-POx/NGQDs may be because of the number of active sites, the small size of particles, and fewer ions recombination. Other materials NiCu-MOF and NiCu-POx, had lower capacity, which might be attributed to a lower number of active sites. Domination of active material causes inaccessibility of surface area for storage of charges due to diffusion. The GCD outcome for NiCu-POx/NGQDs indicates an inverse relationship between potential and time. Because of the synergetic action between both materials, the mixing of pure NGQDs with NiCu-POx increases the capacity of the NiCu-POx/NGQDs [51]. It's worth noting that increased specific capacity was gained with NiCu-POx/NGQDs heterostructure. At 3 mVs−1, the maximum specific capacity calculated using CV curves was 1320 C-g−1, and calculated by GCD curves at 1.0 A g-1 was 1431 C-g−1. The specific capacitance estimated from the CV having scan rate (3 - 50 mVs−1) and GCD curves at current densities (1.0–3.0 A g-1) for each electrode can be seen in Figs. S4(a–d).

The supercapattery was developed with PANI@AC as a cathode while composite NiCu-POx/NGQDs as an anode material. A systematic representation of supercapattery working was shown in Fig. 8 (a). First, we stabilize both electrodes by taking CV measurements for NiCu-POx/NGQDs and PANI doped AC in three-cell assemblies as seen in Fig. 8(b). CV analysis of PANI@AC revealed a rectangular form having nearly zero slope curves, demonstrating the capacitive behaviour of the PANI@AC electrode. As shown in Fig. 8(b), the NiCu-POx/NGQDs were first tested in the three-cell arrangement at potential (0–0.6 V), while the PANI@AC electrode was initially measured individually in a three-cell setup at operating potential (−1.0 – 0 V). The CV profile for PANI@AC exhibited normal capacitive performance. Still, results were obedient for the CV of NiCu-POx/NGQDs, which exhibits faradaic reactions at a higher potential because of its battery-type characteristics. The operational potential for this asymmetric structure (NiCu-POx/NGQDs//PANI@AC) was 0–1.6 V. To explore the device performance, CV calculations were done at different scans (5–100 mVs−1), as shown in Fig. 8(c). Because a supercapattery device combines two separate storing processes, the CV graphs of the prepared hybrid device up to a particular potential showed a semi-rectangular shape. They might vary from this shape with increased potential. This variation from the traditional capacitive form was because of its ion mobility on the electrode surface during redox activities.

Furthermore, there were no noticeable peaks at low potential, suggesting that the (NiCu-POx/NGQDs//PANI@AC) device displayed dominant capacitive performance. And the existence of faradaic chemical processes at large potential was revealed by the larger humps in the curves indicating the battery-graded nature of the device. Furthermore, the form of the CV curves remained unchanged at greater scans, representing the high capacity retention and reliability of the NiCu-POx/NGQDs//PANI@AC device.

Fig. 8(d) displays GCD graphs for the constructed supercapattery (NiCu-POx/NGQDs//PANI@AC). The shape of these GCD graph differed from the conventional triangle form. GCD curves contained small humps due to faradaic reactions that correspond exactly with the CV measurements. GCD graphs at different currents were almost uniform. The specific capacity achieved through this device was 192 Cg-1 from CV calculations (Fig. 8(e)). The maximum specific capacity of 224 Cg-1 was attained at 2.0 A g-1 (Fig. 8(f)). Increasing the current density to 3 A g-1 as seen in Fig. 8(f), the (NiCu-POx/NGQDs//PANI@AC) specific capacity drops to 90 Cg-1. Fig. 8(g) depicts a graph of the EIS spectrum for an asymmetric device. The ESR value for the hybrid device NiCu-POx/NGQDs//PANI@AC was 0.73 U.

The charge storage in the supercapattery (NiCu-POx/NGQDs//PANI@AC) was further described using the theoretical model. The equations below were used to relate current to scan rate.

Fig. 9 (a) showed the theoretically estimated values for b-fitting. The slope of ip vs v graph was used to calculate the b-value. The b-value was used to distinguish battery, capacitive, and supercapattery activity. If the b-value lies in the range of 0–0.5 then the material is battery-graded, a b-value of 0.5–0.8 corresponds to supercapattery nature, and a b-value of 0.8–1.0 belongs to supercapacitors [42]. Fig. 9(b) depicts the graph of the log of current along with a log of scan rate to compute the b-fitting. The calculated b-values for this asymmetric device lied in between 0.5 and 0.8, which was the suggested range of supercapattery as described in the above section [42]. This proves our statement that the formation of supercapattery right and charges storage on this device was because of faradaic (battery-type) and adsorption/desorption processes.

The durability and stability tests for the supercapattery (NiCu-POx/NGQDs//PANI@AC) were critical because they can predict the lifespan of the supercapattery. For this purpose, the prepared supercapattery structure was offered 10,000 charge-discharge repetitions. During the 10,000 rounds, a current of 4.0 A g-1 was sustained. Fig. 9(c) demonstrates the charge-discharge time shown against 10,000 cycles. Initially, electrodes were not activated but once they were activated the 85% specific capacity was retained after completing 10,000 cycles (Fig. 9(d)). The black curve in Fig. 9(d) represents the columbic efficiency, which at the start of the experiment was 100% and after 10,000 repetitions went to 78%, effectively displaying the constant repetition of reversible redox processes.

The two most important features of any storage system are Ed and Pd. The Ed and Pd were measured for this NiCu-POx/NGQDs device from the provided equation.

The supercapattery (NiCu-POx/NGQDs//PANI@AC) showed an energy density of 73 W h-kg−1, sustaining 980 W-kg−1 power, as depicted in Fig. 9(c). The highest specific power obtained with NiCu-POx/NGQDs//AC supercapattery was 3200 Wkg-1, more than the other metallic phosphates. Table 2 further compares our study results to the literature.

Dunn's approach was also used to optimize the capacitive and faradaic contributions at 5, 60, and 100 mV/s, as seen in Fig. 10 (a–c). Dunn's method computes the percentage of charge storage using the following equations.

. The shaded (red) portion in the CV curve represents the capacitive influence. Fig. 10(d–f) showed the matching bar graphs of the capacitive and diffusive percentages for various scan speeds such as 5, 60, and 100 mV/s. At the lower scanning speed of 3 mV/s, the capacitive proportion of 18% and diffusive participation was 82%. Raising the scan rate increases the capacitive participation while decreasing the faradaic contribution. At the greatest scan rate of 100 mV/s, the capacitive proportion was 44%, while the diffusive component was 56%. This implies that at lower scan, the positive electrode (battery grade) had sufficient time to finish the redox process, resulting in a higher diffusive portion. At greater scan rates, the capacitive electrode (negative) had enough time for physical adsorption of ions at the electrode/electrolyte contact and contributes more capacitance to total cell performance. These findings also support our statement about supercapattery formation. The analysis confirmed that increasing the scan rate increases the capacitive participation from 18% to 44% while decreasing the diffusive proportion from 82% to 56%.

Utilizing a linear scan voltammogram in an aqueous electrolyte, various NiCu-based arrangements were tested for their electrocatalytic functions as adjustable electrodes in a standard three-cell configuration. For iR correction, all HER polarization profiles were adjusted. Conventional Pt/C served as a control unit to contrast the results of produced nanostructures (NiCu-MOF, NiCu-POx, and NiCu-POx/NGQDs). Using a three-electrode device, the HER electrochemical properties of Pt/C, NiCu-MOF, NiCu-POx, and NiCu-POx/NGQDs were tested in an alkaline (1 M KOH) electrolyte. The linear sweep polarization profiles of Pt/C, NiCu-MOF, NiCu-POx, and NiCu-POx/NGQDs in the alkaline medium are shown in Fig. 11 (a). In contrast to NiCu-MOF and NiCu-POx, the NiCu-POx/NGQDs demonstrate good HER performance and are even better compared to the HER reference catalyst (Pt/C) beyond −100 mV. For an alkaline solution, the generated Ni–Cu-MOF, NiCu-POx, and NiCu-POx/NGQDs composite electrocatalysts need overpotentials of 151, 182, and 215 mV vs RHE to accomplish current density η10 as indicated in Fig. 11(b). The findings of the NiCu-POx/NGQDs electrocatalytic HER behaviour were viewed as being significantly better than those of other composites, including NiCo2O4 (226 mV) [52], Co2MnO4/NF (−158 mV) [53], and NiCo2O4 (−317 mV) [54]. The increased surface properties of the electrocatalyst significantly contribute to the observed improved electrocatalytic performance of nanospheres on sharp rise domains that were linked and well-organized, stimulating a lot of active edges and a high surface area, which establishes the strongest connections with the electrolyte and achieves efficient electron transmission across the junction [55,56]. The Tafel lines obtained from the polarization of tefel curves, as illustrated in Fig. 11(c), were used to further examine the properties of HER electrocatalysts. NiCu-POx/NGQD have an identical Tafel inclination (58.3 mV dec−1) to Pt/c (55 mV dec−1). The Tafel slope for NiCu-MOF and NiCu-POx was 65.5, 69.2 mV dec−1 which was greater than NiCu-POx/NGQDs, which was sign of fast reaction of Volmer-Heyrovsky [57]. Low Tafel slope for NiCu-POx/NGQDs electrocatalyst in alkaline media demonstrates their exceptional electrocatalytic properties. Table 3 also compared the tefel slope and overpotential of this work with the previous study. The kinetics of the electrocatalytic reaction in alkaline media can follow the following reaction.

The NiCu-POx/NGQDs HER results demonstrated that a porous structure allows for broad-area contact and metallic phase formation. NiCu-POx contributes to the rapid ion/electron transport between the composite electrocatalyst and electrolyte, resulting in increased HER activity.

EIS tests were carried out to clarify the HER electrocatalysts' conductivity characteristics. Fig. 11(d) depicts the EIS curves for Pt/C, NiCu-MOF, NiCu-POx, and NiCu-POx/NGQDs electrocatalysts. The improved interrelationship of the NiCu-POx/NGQDs with the N.F. validates the obtained reduced ohmic resistances (Rs). The high Rct and Rs values obtained for NiCu-MOF and NiCu-POx may result in rough surface characteristics, producing the low HER performance previously described. Furthermore, NiCu-POx/NGQDs composite, as a result of the rapid interaction between ions and electrodes generated due to mixed phase and quantity of active sites, demonstrates higher charge transfer capacities in electrolytes [58]. The quick charge transfer and transit mechanism were also responsible for the high HER efficiency of NiCu-POx/NGQDs, as indicated by the electrochemical impedance spectrum (Fig. 11(d)).

Electrocatalyst endurance was a critical aspect in approving the HER behaviour of hydrothermally generated composites (NiCu-POx/NGQDs). Chronoamperometry (time versus current) measurements were performed for 24 h in an alkaline medium at constant overpotentials of 138 mV vs RHE. The time plotted against the current for NiCu-MOF, Ni–Cu-POx, and NiCu-POx/NGQDs electrocatalyst wa shown in Fig. 11(e). Over the course of 24 h, the NiCu-MOF, Ni–Cu-POx, and NiCu-POx/NGQDs electrocatalyst displays an irregular current density with an initial increase and subsequently a linear fall. This could be due to the interaction of the surface with hydroxide ions. Furthermore, because of less H+ ions, the durability performance altered linearly, which prevents HER reactions. Again the NiCu-Pox/NGQDs outperforms the other two composites by showing stronger stability. This study reveals that NiCu-POx/NGQDs heterostructures have prospects for the development of energy storage technologies and HER applications.

This work describes the hydrothermal synthesis of NiCu-MOF, NiCu-POx, and NiCu-POx/NGQDs for supercappatery and HER applications. The crystallite size was 50–70 nm in size, according to XRD) and SEM. Electrochemical characteristics were also examined for all samples. The NiCu-POx/NGQDs exhibit the highest electrochemical performance showing 1320 C-g−1 capacity at a 3 mVs−1 scan. The estimated capacity through GCD was 1431 C-g−1 at 1.4 A g-1 current in three cell system. The hybrid device (NiCu-POx/NGQDs//PANI@AC) was then fabricated using PANI@AC and NiCu-POx/NGQDs as a negative terminal and positive terminal. The supercapattery (NiCu-POx/NGQDs//PANI@AC) performed well in the electrochemical testing showing the maximum capacity achieved at 2.0 A g-1 was 224 C-g−1. The estimated energy density was 73 W h-kg−1 at the power density of 980 W-kg−1 for this device. The endurance of the NiCu-POx/NGQDs//PANI@AC device was tested by submitting it to 10,000 cycles. It can retain 85% of its initial capacity after completing 10,000 cycles. Additionally, the NiCu-POx/NGQDs showed a small overpotential of 138 mV during the 24 h HER test. This NiCu-POx/NGQD and polyaniline-doped activated carbon demonstrates an innovative method for producing high-performance supercapattery devices.
